name: Fionn mac Cumhaill
description: Fionn mac Cumhaill, also known as Finn MacCool, is a legendary Irish hero and central figure in the Fenian Cycle of Irish mythology. Born as Demne, he gained the name Fionn ('fair' or 'white') after an incident where his hair turned white after burning his thumb while cooking the Salmon of Knowledge. His father was Cumhall, leader of the Fianna, a band of warriors, who was killed before Fionn's birth. Raised in secret to protect him from his father's enemies, Fionn eventually claimed his rightful place as leader of the Fianna. He possessed extraordinary strength, wisdom, and magical abilities. Fionn is renowned for his encounters with giants, monsters, and other supernatural beings. He is credited with creating the Giant's Causeway and battling the fire-breathing Aillen. His wisdom came from tasting the Salmon of Knowledge, granting him the ability to foresee events. Fionn's leadership of the Fianna marked a golden age of Irish heroism, and his stories are filled with bravery, loyalty, and adventure. He is a symbol of Irish strength, cunning, and the enduring power of Celtic mythology.
subtitle: Legendary Leader of the Fianna

keyMyths.title: The Salmon of Knowledge
keyMyths.description: Fionn accidentally gained immense wisdom by burning his thumb while cooking the Salmon of Knowledge for his master, Finegas. He instinctively sucked his thumb to soothe the burn, thus absorbing the salmon's power.

keyMyths.title: Defeating Aillen
keyMyths.description: Fionn defeated the fire-breathing Aillen, who annually burned down Tara, the seat of the High King. He used a spear with a poisoned tip to overcome the monster's magical sleep-inducing music.

keyMyths.title: The Giant's Causeway
keyMyths.description: Fionn, in some versions of the story, built the Giant's Causeway as a pathway to Scotland to confront a giant named Benandonner. The causeway allowed him to cross the sea more easily.

sources.author: Lady Gregory
sources.description: A collection of Irish myths and legends, including stories of Fionn mac Cumhaill.
sources.text: A collection of Irish myths and legends, including stories of Fionn mac Cumhaill.

sources.author: James Macpherson
sources.description: A controversial collection of poems attributed to Fionn's son, Óisín, which popularized Fenian Cycle stories.
